title: The Big Picture
description: One data model. Five access paths: SQL, document API, REST, MongoDB wire protocol, JavaScript. This module sets the context for everything you'll build in the next 85 minutes.
introduction:
Before you write a single query, you need to understand what you're building and why it matters. This workshop uses a financial advisory data model — advisors, clients, accounts, holdings, transactions — because financial services is the domain where the polyglot persistence tax hits hardest. When your client's portfolio is split across a document store for interactions, a relational database for transactions, and a search engine for compliance queries, you don't have an architecture. You have a liability. Every network hop between those systems is latency. Every sync lag is a window for inconsistency. Every additional database is another attack surface, another patch cycle, another team to staff.

The schema you'll work with includes six relational tables (advisors, clients, accounts, holdings, advisor_client_map, and transactions), two JSON collections (client_interactions and advisory_entities), and three JSON Duality Views (client_portfolio_dv, advisor_book_dv, and transaction_feed_dv). That's relational, document, and hybrid access — all in one database, one transaction, one optimizer. The relational tables store the canonical form: normalized, referentially intact, the single source of truth. The JSON collections store interaction logs and single-table design entities as flexible documents. The duality views project the relational truth as rich nested documents — no ETL, no sync, no copies.

You'll access this data through five distinct paths: SQL for analytics and joins, the document API for schemaless CRUD, REST via ORDS for external consumers, the MongoDB wire protocol so existing MongoDB applications connect without driver changes, and JavaScript via node-oracledb for application-layer integration. Five protocols. One database. Same ACID guarantees everywhere.

Underneath all of this is OSON — Oracle's binary JSON format. While MongoDB's BSON uses length-prefixed sequential traversal requiring O(n) field access, OSON uses hash-indexed navigation for O(1) field access regardless of document size or field position. That's not marketing. That's physics. At field position 1000, OSON is 529x faster than BSON. This binary format advantage compounds across every query, every index lookup, every JSON_TRANSFORM operation you'll run in this workshop.

This module has no exercises. It's context. Read the architecture diagram, understand the schema relationships, and internalize the thesis: convergence eliminates the polyglot persistence tax. You don't need five databases. You need one database that speaks five protocols.
checkpoint: By the end of this workshop, you'll have accessed the same data through all five paths — in the same transaction, with the same ACID guarantees.
